skill.name: skill-rails-upgrade
skill.description: Analyze Rails apps and provide upgrade assessments
skill.summary: Analyze Rails apps and provide upgrade assessments
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell-command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review shows the weak-crypto alerts are false positives from normal text, while command and network guidance is expected for a Rails upgrade assistant but can change project state or contact third-party services.

medium_findings.title: External Commands Can Inspect or Modify Projects
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run GitHub CLI, npm, Bundler, Rails, git, and shell commands. These commands are relevant to Rails upgrade work, but they can modify dependencies, create files, run application code, or expose local project metadata if used without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed lines contain concrete command examples for Rails upgrade analysis and execution. The context is legitimate, but command execution against a user repository creates real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Network Lookups Depend on External Services
medium_findings.description: The skill directs agents to fetch Rails release data, official Rails upgrade guidance, Railsdiff results, GitHub release notes, and npm package versions. These lookups are normal for current upgrade advice, but they disclose requested versions or dependency names to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destinations are explicit and related to Rails upgrades. I found no evidence of credential exfiltration, hidden endpoints, or unrelated tracking.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography on lines that contain ordinary description text, Rails documentation URLs, or placeholder wording. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, encryption routines, hashing code, or credential handling in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are visible prose and URLs, not executable cryptographic code. The high-severity static finding does not match the file context.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Access Is Limited to Project Inspection
low_findings.description: The filesystem alerts are shell examples that check expected Rails project files and dependency metadata. This is appropriate for the skill purpose, but users should run it only inside the intended repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads target package.json, importmap, and Rails dependency checks. I found no evidence of broad home-directory scanning or secret-file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many fenced shell commands and inline commands. They are documentation for the agent, not embedded executable scripts, but marketplace users should understand that following the skill may run local commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are directly present in SKILL.md and align with the static external_commands detections. Their purpose is transparent and related to Rails upgrade work.

content.value_statement: Rails upgrades often involve framework defaults, dependency changes, and project-specific configuration risks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a structured assessment and selective merge plan.
actual_capabilities: Verifies that the current workspace appears to be a Rails application.
Finds the current Rails version from Gemfile.lock or Gemfile.
Checks current Rails release information from GitHub and official Rails resources.
Compares the current app with Railsdiff output for relevant version changes.
Reviews Rails-related JavaScript dependencies in package.json or importmap files.
Produces a selective file update plan that protects local customizations.
limitations: Requires access to the Rails project files in the active workspace.
Depends on GitHub, Rails guides, railsdiff.org, or npm for current external data.
Does not guarantee that every application-specific breaking change is detected.
Upgrade execution still needs user review, tests, and deployment validation.

use_cases.title: Plan a Patch Upgrade
use_cases.description: Check whether a Rails patch upgrade is small, safe, and limited to dependency updates.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Major Version Move
use_cases.description: Create a structured assessment before moving across Rails major versions.

use_cases.title: Review Framework Defaults
use_cases.description: Compare local configuration with new Rails defaults before changing application behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Upgrade Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Rails upgrade skill to inspect this project and tell me the current Rails version, latest stable version, upgrade type, and main risks.

prompt_templates.title: Dependency Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Rails app for framework and JavaScript dependency updates needed for the next Rails version. Include npm, importmap, and Rails gem findings.

prompt_templates.title: Selective Merge Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare this app with Railsdiff for the target Rails version and create a selective file update plan. Separate new files, safe updates, manual merges, and skipped changes.

prompt_templates.title: Major Upgrade Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a full Rails major upgrade strategy for this repository. Include risk level, breaking changes, test plan, rollback notes, and staged execution steps.

output_examples.input: Assess my Rails app for an upgrade from 7.1 to the latest stable release.
output: Current version, target version, and upgrade type.
Key Rails guide changes relevant to the version jump.

output_examples.input: Create a selective update plan using Railsdiff.
output: Files safe to update because no local customization was detected.
Files requiring manual merge with local and Rails-side notes.
Changes that can be skipped because they are comments or irrelevant.

output_examples.input: Review JavaScript packages before the Rails upgrade.
output: Rails-related JavaScript packages and current versions.
Recommended updates for Turbo, Stimulus, Action Cable, and Active Storage.
Notes for npm, yarn, or importmap workflows.

best_practices: Run the skill in a clean git worktree so local changes are easy to review.
Ask for an assessment before allowing file updates or dependency changes.
Run the full test suite after each upgrade stage and framework default change.
anti_patterns: Running rails app:update without reviewing local customizations first.
Treating Railsdiff output as a complete replacement for application testing.
Upgrading Rails, gems, and JavaScript packages in one unreviewed change.

faq.question: Does this skill perform the Rails upgrade automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide execution, but it is designed to assess first and ask for review before file changes.

faq.question: Does it use current Rails release information?
faq.answer: Yes. It directs the agent to query GitHub, Rails guides, Railsdiff, and package registries.

faq.question: Can it protect customized configuration files?
faq.answer: It asks the agent to detect local customizations and separate manual merge files from safe updates.

faq.question: Does it support importmap-based Rails apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checks for config/importmap.rb and Rails-related importmap pins.

faq.question: What permissions should users expect?
faq.answer: Users should expect project file reads, external network lookups, and optional local commands.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it in a branch or disposable workspace first. Review every proposed file and dependency change.
